new file mode 100644
index 0000000..1ec5eb7
--- /dev/null
+++ b/CLAUDE.md
@@ -0,0 +1,34 @@
+# Project Guidelines
+
+## Architecture
+- This project follows the repository pattern for data access
+- Services depend on repositories, never on each other directly
+- Events are preferred over direct cross-service calls for decoupling
+- All business logic lives in the service layer
+
+## Coding Conventions
+- Use TypeScript strict mode — no `any` types
+- All public methods must have JSDoc comments
+- Use dependency injection via constructor parameters
+- Error handling: throw typed errors, never return null for errors
+- Prefer async/await over raw Promises
+
+## Testing
+- Tests use vitest
+- Each module has a corresponding test file in tests/
+- Mock external dependencies, test business logic directly
+- Minimum: test the happy path and one error path per public method
+
+## File Organization
+- src/models/ — Data models and interfaces
+- src/repositories/ — Data access layer (implements repository interfaces)
+- src/services/ — Business logic layer
+- src/events/ — Event definitions and event bus
+- src/utils/ — Shared utilities (database, logger, pagination)
+- src/config/ — Configuration files
+- tests/ — Test files mirroring src/ structure
+
+## Git Practices
+- Commit atomically per logical change
+- Write descriptive commit messages explaining the "why"
+- Run tests before committing

--- /dev/null
+++ b/DESIGN.md
@@ -0,0 +1,124 @@
+# Analytics Dashboard API — Design Document
+
+## Overview
+
+This document describes the design for the analytics dashboard feature added to TaskFlow Pro. The analytics layer provides read-only aggregation endpoints over existing order and user data. It is designed as a scaffolding layer — interfaces and stubs are in place; subsequent sessions will implement the real aggregation logic.
+
+## Route Structure
+
+| Method | Path | Handler | Description |
+|--------|------|---------|-------------|
+| GET | `/api/analytics/dashboard` | `handleGetDashboard` | Full dashboard: 4 metric cards, order status breakdown, revenue + order trends |
+| GET | `/api/analytics/metrics/:type` | `handleGetMetricTimeSeries` | Single metric time series with configurable granularity |
+| GET | `/api/analytics/breakdown` | `handleGetBreakdown` | Categorical breakdown by dimension (status, user, product) |
+
+**Why this structure:**
+- `/dashboard` is a composite endpoint — one call populates the entire dashboard UI, reducing round trips.
+- `/metrics/:type` allows drilling into any single metric with custom date ranges and granularity, supporting chart zoom and exploration.
+- `/breakdown` is a separate endpoint because breakdown dimensions are orthogonal to time-series metrics and may be used independently (e.g., a status pie chart).
+
+All endpoints are GET (read-only). No mutations. Query parameters handle filtering.
+
+## Data Models
+
+All types are in `src/models/analytics.ts`.
+
+### Core Types
+
+- **`TimeSeriesPoint`** — `{ timestamp, value, label? }` — atomic unit for all charts. Uses ISO 8601 strings for timestamps to avoid serialization issues.
+- **`MetricSummary`** — `{ metricType, currentValue, previousValue, changePercent, trend, formattedValue }` — powers dashboard summary cards. Includes period-over-period comparison so the UI can show up/down arrows.
+- **`BreakdownItem`** — `{ category, value, percentage }` — a single row in a categorical chart (pie, bar).
+- **`DashboardData`** — composite response containing `metrics[]`, `ordersByStatus[]`, `recentOrdersTrend[]`, `revenueTrend[]`, and `generatedAt`.
+
+### Query Types
+
+- **`DashboardQuery`** — optional `startDate`/`endDate` (defaults to last 30 days).
+- **`MetricQuery`** — required `metricType`, `startDate`, `endDate`; optional `granularity` (hour/day/week/month, default: day).
+- **`BreakdownQuery`** — required `dimension` (status/user/product); optional date range and `limit`.
+
+### Why These Types
+
+- **Separate query/response types** follow the existing Input/Entity pattern (e.g., `CreateOrderInput` vs `Order`).
+- **`MetricType` as a union type** provides compile-time safety and makes it easy to add new metrics later.
+- **`TimeGranularity`** supports the most common dashboard zoom levels without over-engineering.
+
+## Service Design (`AnalyticsService`)
+
+Located at `src/services/analytics.service.ts`. Follows existing service patterns:
+- Constructor injection of `OrderRepository` and `UserRepository`
+- Private `Logger` instance
+- Synchronous methods (no event emission — this is read-only)
+
+### Methods
+
+| Method | Input | Output | Notes |
+|--------|-------|--------|-------|
+| `getDashboard(query?)` | `DashboardQuery` | `DashboardData` | Composite; calls internal helpers |
+| `getMetricTimeSeries(query)` | `MetricQuery` | `MetricTimeSeriesResponse` | Single metric drill-down |
+| `getBreakdown(query)` | `BreakdownQuery` | `BreakdownResponse` | Categorical grouping |
+
+### Implementation Guide for Next Developer
+
+1. **`getDashboard`**: Query all orders and users within the date range. For each `MetricType`, compute current and previous period values. Build daily `TimeSeriesPoint[]` arrays by bucketing `order.createdAt`. Group orders by `order.status` for the breakdown.
+
+2. **`getMetricTimeSeries`**: Similar to dashboard but for one metric. Create time buckets based on `granularity`. For each bucket, sum/count the relevant data. For "previous period" comparison, query the same duration immediately before `startDate`.
+
+3. **`getBreakdown`**: 
+   - `status`: group orders by `order.status`, count each.
+   - `user`: group orders by `order.userId`, sum `order.total`, join user name.
+   - `product`: flatten `order.items`, group by `productId`, sum quantities.
+
+4. **Performance considerations**: The in-memory `Database` makes full scans cheap. If this moves to a real database, add date-range indexes and consider materialized views or caching for the dashboard endpoint.
+
+## Route Handlers
+
+Located at `src/routes/analytics.routes.ts`. Since this project has no HTTP framework, handlers are plain functions:
+
+```typescript
+function handleGetDashboard(service: AnalyticsService, queryParams: Record<string, string | undefined>): DashboardData
+```
+
+Each handler:
+- Takes the `AnalyticsService` instance and parsed params
+- Validates inputs (metric type, required fields, granularity)
+- Throws descriptive `Error` on invalid input
+- Returns the typed response directly
+
+To wire to a framework, create a thin adapter:
+```typescript
+// Express example
+app.get('/api/analytics/dashboard', (req, res) => {
+  try {
+    res.json(handleGetDashboard(analyticsService, req.query));
+  } catch (err) {
+    res.status(400).json({ error: err.message });
+  }
+});
+```
+
+## Architectural Decisions
+
+1. **No new repository** — Analytics reads from existing `OrderRepository` and `UserRepository`. No separate analytics data store since we're aggregating existing data, not storing analytics events.
+
+2. **Framework-agnostic handlers** — The codebase is a pure TS library with no web framework. Route handlers are typed functions, not Express middleware. This preserves the library architecture and lets any future integration choose its own framework.
+
+3. **Synchronous service methods** — Unlike `OrderService.createOrder` (async due to event emission), analytics methods are synchronous. They only read data, emit no events, and the in-memory database is synchronous. If the data layer becomes async (e.g., real DB), these signatures should change to `Promise<T>`.
+
+4. **Placeholder data pattern** — Stubs return zero-valued but correctly-typed data. This lets downstream sessions focus on aggregation logic without worrying about type shape, and means the scaffolding compiles and can be tested for structure immediately.
+
+## File Map
+
+| File | Purpose |
+|------|---------|
+| `src/models/analytics.ts` | All analytics type definitions |
+| `src/services/analytics.service.ts` | Aggregation service with stub implementations |
+| `src/routes/analytics.routes.ts` | Route definitions and handler functions |
+| `src/index.ts` | Updated with analytics exports |
+
+## What's Left to Build
+
+- [ ] Implement real aggregation in `AnalyticsService` (replace placeholder data)
+- [ ] Add unit tests for `AnalyticsService` in `tests/services/analytics.service.test.ts`
+- [ ] Add unit tests for route handlers in `tests/routes/analytics.routes.test.ts`
+- [ ] Consider adding an `AnalyticsEvent` type to `event-types.ts` if analytics queries should be auditable
+- [ ] Wire handlers to an HTTP framework if/when one is added to the project

diff --git a/src/services/analytics.service.ts b/src/services/analytics.service.ts
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..a0981b9
--- /dev/null
+++ b/src/services/analytics.service.ts
@@ -0,0 +1,171 @@
+/**
+ * Analytics service — aggregation and computation logic for the analytics dashboard.
+ *
+ * Depends on:
+ * - OrderRepository (data layer) for order data
+ * - UserRepository (data layer) for user data
+ *
+ * Architecture notes:
+ * - This service reads from existing repositories; it does NOT write data.
+ * - All methods return typed analytics models (see src/models/analytics.ts).
+ * - Current implementation returns placeholder data. The next developer should
+ *   replace stub bodies with real aggregation logic using repository queries.
+ * - Time-range filtering and granularity bucketing are the core work to implement.
+ *
+ * Implementation guide for the next developer:
+ * 1. getDashboard: Query orders + users within the date range, compute summaries,
+ *    build time series by bucketing order.createdAt into daily bins.
+ * 2. getMetricTimeSeries: Similar to dashboard but for a single metric,
+ *    with configurable granularity (hour/day/week/month).
+ * 3. getBreakdown: Group orders by the requested dimension and compute counts/totals.
+ * 4. Consider caching expensive aggregations if the dataset grows large.
+ */
+
+import type { OrderRepository } from '../repositories/order.repository.js';
+import type { UserRepository } from '../repositories/user.repository.js';
+import type {
+  DashboardData,
+  DashboardQuery,
+  MetricQuery,
+  MetricTimeSeriesResponse,
+  BreakdownQuery,
+  BreakdownResponse,
+  MetricSummary,
+  TimeSeriesPoint,
+  BreakdownItem,
+  TimeGranularity,
+} from '../models/analytics.js';
+import { Logger } from '../utils/logger.js';
+
+export class AnalyticsService {
+  private logger = new Logger('AnalyticsService');
+
+  constructor(
+    private orderRepository: OrderRepository,
+    private userRepository: UserRepository,
+  ) {}
+
+  /**
+   * Get the full dashboard view with summary metrics, breakdowns, and trends.
+   *
+   * TODO: Replace placeholder data with real aggregation:
+   * - Query all orders within [startDate, endDate]
+   * - Compute revenue, order count, user count, and AOV summaries
+   * - Build daily time series for revenue and order trends
+   * - Group orders by status for the breakdown
+   */
+  getDashboard(query: DashboardQuery = {}): DashboardData {
+    this.logger.info('Generating dashboard data');
+
+    const startDate = query.startDate ?? new Date(Date.now() - 30 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000).toISOString();
+    const endDate = query.endDate ?? new Date().toISOString();
+
+    // Placeholder: downstream sessions will implement real aggregation
+    const metrics: MetricSummary[] = [
+      this.buildPlaceholderSummary('revenue'),
+      this.buildPlaceholderSummary('orders'),
+      this.buildPlaceholderSummary('users'),
+      this.buildPlaceholderSummary('average-order-value'),
+    ];
+
+    const ordersByStatus: BreakdownItem[] = [
+      { category: 'pending', value: 0, percentage: 0 },
+      { category: 'confirmed', value: 0, percentage: 0 },
+      { category: 'shipped', value: 0, percentage: 0 },
+      { category: 'delivered', value: 0, percentage: 0 },
+      { category: 'cancelled', value: 0, percentage: 0 },
+    ];
+
+    const recentOrdersTrend = this.buildPlaceholderSeries(startDate, endDate, 'day');
+    const revenueTrend = this.buildPlaceholderSeries(startDate, endDate, 'day');
+
+    return {
+      metrics,
+      ordersByStatus,
+      recentOrdersTrend,
+      revenueTrend,
+      generatedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
+    };
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Get time series data for a single metric.
+   *
+   * TODO: Replace placeholder with real aggregation:
+   * - Query relevant data (orders for revenue/orders/AOV, users for users)
+   * - Bucket by granularity
+   * - Compute the summary by comparing current vs previous period
+   */
+  getMetricTimeSeries(query: MetricQuery): MetricTimeSeriesResponse {
+    this.logger.info(`Generating time series for metric: ${query.metricType}`);
+
+    const granularity = query.granularity ?? 'day';
+
+    return {
+      metricType: query.metricType,
+      summary: this.buildPlaceholderSummary(query.metricType),
+      series: this.buildPlaceholderSeries(query.startDate, query.endDate, granularity),
+      query: {
+        startDate: query.startDate,
+        endDate: query.endDate,
+        granularity,
+      },
+    };
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Get a categorical breakdown (e.g., orders by status, by user, by product).
+   *
+   * TODO: Replace placeholder with real aggregation:
+   * - For 'status': group orders by status, count each
+   * - For 'user': group orders by userId, sum totals, join user names
+   * - For 'product': group order items by productId, sum quantities
+   */
+  getBreakdown(query: BreakdownQuery): BreakdownResponse {
+    this.logger.info(`Generating breakdown by: ${query.dimension}`);
+
+    const startDate = query.startDate ?? new Date(Date.now() - 30 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000).toISOString();
+    const endDate = query.endDate ?? new Date().toISOString();
+    const limit = query.limit ?? 10;
+
+    return {
+      dimension: query.dimension,
+      items: [],
+      total: 0,
+      query: {
+        startDate,
+        endDate,
+        limit,
+      },
+    };
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Build a placeholder MetricSummary. Replace with real computation.
+   */
+  private buildPlaceholderSummary(metricType: MetricSummary['metricType']): MetricSummary {
+    return {
+      metricType,
+      currentValue: 0,
+      previousValue: 0,
+      changePercent: 0,
+      trend: 'flat',
+      formattedValue: '0',
+    };
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Build a placeholder time series between two dates. Replace with real bucketing.
+   */
+  private buildPlaceholderSeries(
+    _startDate: string,
+    _endDate: string,
+    _granularity: TimeGranularity,
+  ): TimeSeriesPoint[] {
+    // Placeholder: return empty series. Real implementation should:
+    // 1. Parse start/end dates
+    // 2. Create buckets based on granularity
+    // 3. Aggregate data into each bucket
+    return [];
+  }
+}

This is a TypeScript service (~1,140 lines, ~24 files). You'll be adding a new analytics feature. Follow existing patterns:

Directory structure:

src/

tests/           # Test files
```

Your task: Design and scaffold the API for a new analytics dashboard. Create the route structure, data models, and write a brief design doc.

Specifically:
1. Design the analytics dashboard API: decide on routes (e.g., GET /api/analytics/dashboard, GET /api/analytics/metrics/:type), request/response shapes, and data flow.
2. Create TypeScript interfaces/types for analytics data models (e.g., DashboardData, MetricSummary, TimeSeriesPoint) in `src/models/` or `src/types/`.

Important:
- Focus on DESIGN over implementation — the next sessions will implement the actual logic.
- Create clear interfaces that downstream sessions can implement against.
- Document your decisions thoroughly — 4 more developers will build on your foundation.

parameters.content:
# Analytics Dashboard API — Design Document

## Overview

This document describes the design for the analytics dashboard feature added to TaskFlow Pro. The analytics layer provides read-only aggregation endpoints over existing order and user data. It is designed as a scaffolding layer — interfaces and stubs are in place; subsequent sessions will implement the real aggregation logic.

## Route Structure

| Method | Path | Handler | Description |
|--------|------|---------|-------------|
| GET | `/api/analytics/dashboard` | `handleGetDashboard` | Full dashboard: 4 metric cards, order status breakdown, revenue + order trends |
| GET | `/api/analytics/metrics/:type` | `handleGetMetricTimeSeries` | Single metric time series with configurable granularity |
| GET | `/api/analytics/breakdown` | `handleGetBreakdown` | Categorical breakdown by dimension (status, user, product) |

**Why this structure:**
- `/dashboard` is a composite endpoint — one call populates the entire dashboard UI, reducing round trips.
- `/metrics/:type` allows drilling into any single metric with custom date ranges and granularity, supporting chart zoom and exploration.
- `/breakdown` is a separate endpoint because breakdown dimensions are orthogonal to time-series metrics and may be used independently (e.g., a status pie chart).

All endpoints are GET (read-only). No mutations. Query parameters handle filtering.

## Data Models

All types are in `src/models/analytics.ts`.

### Core Types

- **`TimeSeriesPoint`** — `{ timestamp, value, label? }` — atomic unit for all charts. Uses ISO 8601 strings for timestamps to avoid serialization issues.
- **`MetricSummary`** — `{ metricType, currentValue, previousValue, changePercent, trend, formattedValue }` — powers dashboard summary cards. Includes period-over-period comparison so the UI can show up/down arrows.
- **`BreakdownItem`** — `{ category, value, percentage }` — a single row in a categorical chart (pie, bar).
- **`DashboardData`** — composite response containing `metrics[]`, `ordersByStatus[]`, `recentOrdersTrend[]`, `revenueTrend[]`, and `generatedAt`.

### Query Types

- **`DashboardQuery`** — optional `startDate`/`endDate` (defaults to last 30 days).
- **`MetricQuery`** — required `metricType`, `startDate`, `endDate`; optional `granularity` (hour/day/week/month, default: day).
- **`BreakdownQuery`** — required `dimension` (status/user/product); optional date range and `limit`.

### Why These Types

- **Separate query/response types** follow the existing Input/Entity pattern (e.g., `CreateOrderInput` vs `Order`).
- **`MetricType` as a union type** provides compile-time safety and makes it easy to add new metrics later.
- **`TimeGranularity`** supports the most common dashboard zoom levels without over-engineering.

## Service Design (`AnalyticsService`)

Located at `src/services/analytics.service.ts`. Follows existing service patterns:
- Constructor injection of `OrderRepository` and `UserRepository`
- Private `Logger` instance
- Synchronous methods (no event emission — this is read-only)

### Methods

| Method | Input | Output | Notes |
|--------|-------|--------|-------|
| `getDashboard(query?)` | `DashboardQuery` | `DashboardData` | Composite; calls internal helpers |
| `getMetricTimeSeries(query)` | `MetricQuery` | `MetricTimeSeriesResponse` | Single metric drill-down |
| `getBreakdown(query)` | `BreakdownQuery` | `BreakdownResponse` | Categorical grouping |

### Implementation Guide for Next Developer

1. **`getDashboard`**: Query all orders and users within the date range. For each `MetricType`, compute current and previous period values. Build daily `TimeSeriesPoint[]` arrays by bucketing `order.createdAt`. Group orders by `order.status` for the breakdown.

2. **`getMetricTimeSeries`**: Similar to dashboard but for one metric. Create time buckets based on `granularity`. For each bucket, sum/count the relevant data. For "previous period" comparison, query the same duration immediately before `startDate`.

3. **`getBreakdown`**: 
   - `status`: group orders by `order.status`, count each.
   - `user`: group orders by `order.userId`, sum `order.total`, join user name.
   - `product`: flatten `order.items`, group by `productId`, sum quantities.

4. **Performance considerations**: The in-memory `Database` makes full scans cheap. If this moves to a real database, add date-range indexes and consider materialized views or caching for the dashboard endpoint.

## Route Handlers

Located at `src/routes/analytics.routes.ts`. Since this project has no HTTP framework, handlers are plain functions:

```typescript
function handleGetDashboard(service: AnalyticsService, queryParams: Record<string, string | undefined>): DashboardData
```

Each handler:
- Takes the `AnalyticsService` instance and parsed params
- Validates inputs (metric type, required fields, granularity)
- Throws descriptive `Error` on invalid input
- Returns the typed response directly

To wire to a framework, create a thin adapter:
```typescript
// Express example
app.get('/api/analytics/dashboard', (req, res) => {
  try {
    res.json(handleGetDashboard(analyticsService, req.query));
  } catch (err) {
    res.status(400).json({ error: err.message });
  }
});
```

## Architectural Decisions

1. **No new repository** — Analytics reads from existing `OrderRepository` and `UserRepository`. No separate analytics data store since we're aggregating existing data, not storing analytics events.

2. **Framework-agnostic handlers** — The codebase is a pure TS library with no web framework. Route handlers are typed functions, not Express middleware. This preserves the library architecture and lets any future integration choose its own framework.

3. **Synchronous service methods** — Unlike `OrderService.createOrder` (async due to event emission), analytics methods are synchronous. They only read data, emit no events, and the in-memory database is synchronous. If the data layer becomes async (e.g., real DB), these signatures should change to `Promise<T>`.

4. **Placeholder data pattern** — Stubs return zero-valued but correctly-typed data. This lets downstream sessions focus on aggregation logic without worrying about type shape, and means the scaffolding compiles and can be tested for structure immediately.

## File Map

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `src/models/analytics.ts` | All analytics type definitions |
| `src/services/analytics.service.ts` | Aggregation service with stub implementations |
| `src/routes/analytics.routes.ts` | Route definitions and handler functions |
| `src/index.ts` | Updated with analytics exports |

## What's Left to Build

- [ ] Implement real aggregation in `AnalyticsService` (replace placeholder data)
- [ ] Add unit tests for `AnalyticsService` in `tests/services/analytics.service.test.ts`
- [ ] Add unit tests for route handlers in `tests/routes/analytics.routes.test.ts`
- [ ] Consider adding an `AnalyticsEvent` type to `event-types.ts` if analytics queries should be auditable
- [ ] Wire handlers to an HTTP framework if/when one is added to the project

Write DESIGN.md with architecture decisions
Verify compilation with tsc

parameters.detail:
Created 3 new files + updated index.ts. All compile cleanly (tsc --noEmit passes).

Files created:
- src/models/analytics.ts — 12 types/interfaces covering all API contracts
- src/services/analytics.service.ts — AnalyticsService with 3 stub methods (getDashboard, getMetricTimeSeries, getBreakdown)
- src/routes/analytics.routes.ts — 3 framework-agnostic handler functions with input validation
- DESIGN.md — Full design doc with route structure, data model rationale, implementation guide

Key design decisions:
1. Framework-agnostic handlers (no Express/Fastify added)
2. No new repository — analytics reads from existing OrderRepository + UserRepository
3. Synchronous service methods (read-only, no events)
4. Placeholder data returns correctly-typed zero values

What's left for subsequent agents:
- Implement real aggregation logic in AnalyticsService (replace stubs)
- Add tests for service and route handlers
- Consider event types for analytics if audit trail needed
parameters.summary: Agent 1 complete: Analytics API designed and scaffolded — models, service stubs, route handlers, design doc
